report_metadata.cancer_type: Oral Tongue Mucoepidermoid Carcinoma (Minor Salivary Gland Origin, High-Grade)
molecular_profile: HER2 IHC 2+ (FISH confirmation pending — T-DXd candidate if amplified)
Minor salivary gland origin — NOT squamous cell carcinoma

treatments.name: Wide Local Excision with Adequate Margins + Elective Neck Dissection (Levels I-IV)

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN and ASCO guidelines (2025) strongly recommend primary surgical resection with wide margins for resectable salivary gland malignancies. Extensive retrospective data supports surgery as the definitive treatment for T2N0 high-grade MEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Complete surgical resection with clear margins is the single most important prognostic factor. 5-year OS for adequately resected high-grade MEC ranges 50-67%. Negative margins convert a poor prognosis to a manageable one.

accessibility.rationale: Widely available at any head and neck surgical center. Standard procedure for oral tongue tumors. No special equipment or biomarker requirements.

safety_profile.rationale: Surgical morbidity includes potential speech and swallowing impairment, tongue mobility restriction, and neck dissection complications (shoulder dysfunction, nerve injury). Manageable with rehabilitation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability — surgery is indicated regardless of molecular profile. The procedure addresses the primary tumor independent of fusion status, HER2, or AR status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision removes the primary tumor with adequate margins (ideally >5mm). Elective neck dissection addresses the 30-50% risk of occult nodal metastasis in high-grade MEC, even in clinically N0 disease. NCCN recommends levels II-IV dissection for high-grade tumors.

key_evidence.study_name: NCCN Head and Neck Cancer Guidelines v2.2025 / ASCO Salivary Gland Malignancy Guideline

notable_side_effects: Shoulder dysfunction (from neck dissection — spinal accessory nerve)

treatments.name: Adjuvant Postoperative Radiation Therapy (60-66 Gy IMRT)

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN and ASCO guidelines recommend postoperative RT for high-grade salivary gland tumors. Large retrospective series demonstrate improved locoregional control. No randomized trials specific to MEC, but level 2B evidence from multiple institutional series.

survival_benefit.rationale: Adjuvant RT reduces locoregional recurrence significantly. Series from MD Anderson and Memorial Sloan Kettering show 5-year locoregional control of 85-95% with surgery + RT vs 50-60% with surgery alone for high-grade salivary tumors. NCDB analysis confirms OS benefit for adjuvant RT.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT is widely available at most radiation oncology centers. Standard 6-7 week course. Well-established treatment planning protocols for oral cavity tumors.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant acute and late toxicities in the oral cavity: mucositis (grade 2+ in up to 77%), xerostomia (up to 62%), taste changes, dental complications, osteoradionecrosis risk, and fibrosis. Manageable with supportive care.

biomarker_match.rationale: Indicated for all high-grade MEC regardless of molecular profile. NCCN lists high-grade histology as an independent indication for adjuvant RT even with negative margins.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-energy photon radiation damages DNA in residual microscopic tumor cells, inducing apoptosis and preventing locoregional recurrence. IMRT technique allows conformal dose delivery to tumor bed and elective nodal regions while minimizing dose to contralateral structures. Doses of 60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions target the primary site; 54-60 Gy to elective nodal volumes.

key_evidence.study_name: Adjuvant RT for Major Salivary Gland Malignancies — Multi-institutional Analysis

treatments.name: Larotrectinib / Entrectinib (NTRK Fusion-Directed — If NTRK Fusion Detected)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase I/II basket trials with robust data: Larotrectinib 92% ORR in 24 NTRK+ salivary gland patients (13% CR, 79% PR). Entrectinib 57-86% ORR. Both FDA-approved tumor-agnostic for NTRK fusion-positive cancers. However, NTRK fusions are rare in non-secretory MEC (~4% of SGC).

survival_benefit.rationale: Larotrectinib: median OS 40.7 months across all NTRK+ tumors. Rapid responses (median 1.84 months to response). Durable responses observed. Entrectinib: median PFS 13.8 months, median OS 37.1 months. Transformative for fusion-positive patients.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs FDA-approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (larotrectinib 2018, entrectinib 2019). Oral administration. Requires NGS or FISH testing to identify NTRK fusion. Covered by most insurance if fusion-positive.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated. Most treatment-related AEs are grade 1-2. Larotrectinib: fatigue, dizziness, nausea, constipation. Entrectinib: dysgeusia, fatigue, weight gain, cognitive effects. Manageable with dose adjustments.

biomarker_match.rationale: NTRK fusions are rare in mucoepidermoid carcinoma (approximately 4% of all SGC, predominantly in secretory carcinoma subtype). MEC accounted for only 13% of NTRK+ SGC in larotrectinib series. Patient should undergo NGS testing, but probability of NTRK fusion is low.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: TRK inhibitors selectively block the kinase activity of tropomyosin receptor kinases (TRKA/B/C) encoded by NTRK1/2/3 genes. In tumors driven by NTRK gene fusions, TRK proteins are constitutively activated, driving oncogenic signaling. Larotrectinib and entrectinib inhibit this aberrant signaling, inducing tumor regression. Entrectinib additionally inhibits ROS1 and ALK.

key_evidence.study_name: Larotrectinib Phase I/II in TRK Fusion-Positive Salivary Gland Cancers
key_evidence.journal: The Oncologist / New England Journal of Medicine

biomarker_requirements: NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion (confirmed by NGS, FISH, or IHC screening)
Probability of NTRK fusion is low in non-secretory MEC (~4% of SGC)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (Tumor-Agnostic for NTRK Fusion-Positive Solid Tumors)

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Adjuvant, as Alternative to IMRT)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II data and multiple institutional series demonstrate excellent locoregional control (95% at 3 years) with significantly reduced toxicity compared to IMRT. No randomized comparison to photon RT exists for salivary gland tumors, but dosimetric advantages are well established.

survival_benefit.rationale: 3-year OS of 96.1% and PFS of 80.7% in proton therapy series for major salivary gland cancers. Equivalent tumor control to IMRT with substantially reduced collateral damage to oral cavity structures — critical for oral tongue tumors.

accessibility.rationale: Limited availability — approximately 40 proton centers in the US. Requires referral, often with significant travel. Insurance coverage improving but may require prior authorization. Higher cost than photon IMRT.

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly superior toxicity profile vs IMRT: mucositis grade 2+ only 17% vs 52% (p=0.02), nausea 11% vs 57% (p=0.003), dysgeusia 6% vs 65% (p<0.001). No late grade 3+ toxicities reported. Particularly advantageous for oral tongue to preserve contralateral salivary function.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability as radiation modality. Benefit is dosimetric, not biology-dependent. Especially advantageous for oral cavity tumors where sparing contralateral structures and minimizing oral mucositis is critical.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beam therapy delivers radiation via charged particles with a Bragg peak, depositing maximum dose at the tumor target with minimal exit dose. This spares contralateral oral cavity, parotid glands, submandibular glands, and brainstem. For unilateral oral tongue tumors, PBT reduces mean contralateral parotid dose from 1.4 Gy (IMRT) to 0 Gy, and mean oral cavity dose from 21 Gy to <1 Gy.

key_evidence.study_name: Proton Therapy for Major Salivary Gland Cancer: Clinical Outcomes

treatments.name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan (T-DXd / Enhertu) — If HER2 FISH-Amplified

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II DESTINY-PanTumor02 trial showed 41.7% ORR in salivary gland tumors. MYTHOS phase II trial specific to HER2+ salivary gland cancer. Pooled phase I analysis of 17 patients showed 58.8% ORR. FDA-approved for HER2+ solid tumors (tumor-agnostic approval August 2024).

survival_benefit.rationale: Pooled phase I: median PFS 20.5 months, median DOR 17.6 months. DESTINY-PanTumor02 HER2 IHC 3+ cohort: median PFS 11.9 months, median OS 21.1 months. Durable responses (some >20 months) observed in salivary gland subset.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved (accelerated) for HER2 IHC 3+ solid tumors (August 2024). Requires FISH confirmation of HER2 amplification for this patient (IHC 2+). If FISH+, drug is accessible via standard oncology prescription. Requires infusion center.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant toxicity profile: grade 3+ AEs in 40.8% (PanTumor02). ILD/pneumonitis in 10.5-29.4% including fatal cases. Neutropenia grade 3+ in 52.9%. Nausea in 88.2%. Treatment discontinuation in 35.3% due to AEs. Requires close ILD monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient is HER2 IHC 2+ — requires FISH confirmation. If FISH-amplified, qualifies for FDA-approved indication. If FISH-negative (HER2-low), may still have modest benefit but data is limited in salivary gland cancers for HER2-low.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) consisting of anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody linked to topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (deruxtecan/DXd). Binds HER2 on tumor cell surface, internalizes, and releases cytotoxic payload intracellularly. Bystander killing effect extends activity to HER2-low tumor cells via membrane-permeable DXd released from dying HER2+ cells.

key_evidence.study_name: DESTINY-PanTumor02 Phase II / Pooled Phase I Salivary Gland Analysis
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / Japanese Journal of Clinical Oncology

biomarker_requirements: HER2 IHC 3+ (FDA approved) or IHC 2+/FISH+ (amplified)
Patient requires FISH confirmation of HER2 amplification (currently IHC 2+)
notable_side_effects: Interstitial lung disease / pneumonitis (10.5-29.4%, including fatal cases)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (Tumor-Agnostic, HER2 IHC 3+ Solid Tumors, August 2024)

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase Ib KEYNOTE-028 (26 PD-L1+ patients, 12% ORR) and Phase II KEYNOTE-158 data available. Limited sample size in salivary gland cohorts. One remarkable case report of complete pathological response in MEC specifically. MEC has immune-inflamed phenotype favoring ICI response.

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-028: 12% ORR with median DOR of 4 months. Meta-analysis of 4 trials (180 patients): complete/partial response 5.6%, disease control rate 58.4%. Durable responses observed in responders but low overall response rate limits population-level benefit.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for multiple indications including MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors and TMB-high tumors (tumor-agnostic). Would require PD-L1 testing, MSI, or TMB assessment. Widely available at oncology centers. Would be used in recurrent/metastatic setting.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-established safety profile. Immune-related adverse events are manageable: thyroiditis, colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, dermatitis. Lower toxicity than chemotherapy. Grade 3+ irAEs in approximately 10-15% of patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: MEC exhibits immune-inflamed phenotype, suggesting higher ICI responsiveness than other salivary subtypes. PD-L1 status, MSI, and TMB should be tested. CRTC1-MAML2 fusion-negative high-grade MEC may have higher mutation burden. AR-negative status does not affect ICI eligibility.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the interaction between PD-1 receptor on T cells and PD-L1/PD-L2 ligands on tumor cells, restoring anti-tumor immune surveillance. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma has an immune-inflamed tumor microenvironment with higher T-cell infiltration compared to other salivary subtypes, potentially enhancing checkpoint inhibitor efficacy.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-028 Phase Ib / KEYNOTE-158 Phase II
key_evidence.journal: American Journal of Clinical Oncology / European Journal of Cancer

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS testing recommended
MSI/dMMR and TMB testing recommended
Tumor-agnostic approval if MSI-H or TMB >= 10 mut/Mb
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (tumor-agnostic for MSI-H/TMB-H; salivary gland use off-label unless biomarker-selected)

treatments.name: Trastuzumab + Pertuzumab (Dual HER2 Blockade — If HER2 Amplified)

evidence_level.rationale: MyPathway basket trial: 16 HER2+ non-ACC salivary gland cancer patients, 56% ORR. Phase II data. Primarily studied in salivary duct carcinoma rather than MEC specifically. HER2 amplification is prerequisite.

survival_benefit.rationale: MyPathway: median PFS 9.1 months, median OS 20.4 months. Meaningful disease control in HER2+ SGC. Duration of benefit moderate. Data predominantly from SDC which has higher HER2+ rates than MEC.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs FDA-approved for HER2+ breast cancer. Used off-label in HER2+ SGC. Requires FISH-confirmed HER2 amplification. Available at standard oncology infusion centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-established safety profile from breast cancer experience. Cardiotoxicity risk requires LVEF monitoring. Diarrhea, nausea, fatigue. Lower toxicity than ADCs or chemotherapy. Infusion reactions possible.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires HER2 amplification (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/FISH+). Patient is IHC 2+ — needs FISH confirmation. If FISH-negative, not a candidate. HER2 amplification in MEC is 0-38% depending on series. T-DXd may be preferred if available.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Trastuzumab binds HER2 domain IV, inhibiting ligand-independent HER2 signaling and mediating antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC). Pertuzumab binds HER2 domain II, blocking HER2-HER3 dimerization. Dual blockade provides more complete HER2 pathway suppression than either agent alone.

key_evidence.study_name: MyPathway Basket Trial — HER2+ Salivary Gland Cancer Cohort
key_evidence.journal: The Lancet Oncology / Journal of Clinical Oncology

biomarker_requirements: HER2 amplification (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/FISH+)
Patient requires FISH confirmation (currently IHC 2+)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (off-label for salivary gland cancer)

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (Dual Checkpoint Inhibition)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial (Nature Medicine 2023): 64 patients, non-ACC cohort met primary endpoint with 16% ORR (5/32). Published in high-impact journal. MEC included in non-ACC cohort. Separate phase II study (Cancer Medicine 2025) confirmed activity in non-ACC salivary gland cancers.

survival_benefit.rationale: Non-ACC cohort: 16% ORR with median PFS 2.2 months. Modest but meaningful for refractory salivary gland cancers. Responses may be more durable than chemotherapy. Limited to recurrent/metastatic setting — no adjuvant data.

accessibility.rationale: Both nivolumab and ipilimumab are FDA-approved for multiple tumor types. Combination is used off-label for salivary gland cancers. Requires specialized oncology center familiar with dual ICI management and irAE monitoring.

safety_profile.rationale: Dual ICI carries higher toxicity than single-agent: grade 3+ treatment-related AEs in 38% (24/64 patients). Immune-related events include colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies. Requires experienced irAE management.

biomarker_match.rationale: MEC immune-inflamed phenotype may favor dual checkpoint blockade over single-agent. Non-ACC salivary tumors showed better responses than ACC. CRTC1-MAML2 fusion-negative status and potential higher mutational burden may enhance immunogenicity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) blocks PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint while ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) enhances T-cell priming and activation in lymph nodes. Dual blockade provides complementary immune activation: CTLA-4 inhibition expands the T-cell repertoire while PD-1 blockade sustains anti-tumor T-cell activity in the tumor microenvironment.

key_evidence.study_name: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab in Advanced Salivary Gland Cancer: Phase 2 Trial

biomarker_requirements: Non-ACC salivary gland histology (MEC qualifies)
PD-L1 testing recommended but not required

treatments.name: Adjuvant Concurrent Chemoradiation (Cisplatin + RT)
treatments.category: Standard of Care (Investigational for Salivary Gland)

evidence_level.rationale: No completed randomized trial for salivary gland cancers. RTOG 1008 (NCT01220583) is the landmark phase II/III trial comparing RT+cisplatin vs RT alone — closed to accrual, results pending. Retrospective data show mixed results: some series show improved locoregional control, others show no survival benefit.

survival_benefit.rationale: Taiwan Cancer Registry study found no significant difference in OS or DSS between CRT and RT alone. However, some institutional series report promising locoregional control (median survival 42 months). Extrapolation from HNSCC data suggests potential benefit for high-grade tumors but remains unproven in salivary histologies.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin is widely available, inexpensive, and well-established in head and neck oncology. Standard concurrent dosing (100mg/m2 q3 weeks x3 or weekly 40mg/m2) is routine at most oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Adding cisplatin to RT significantly increases toxicity: severe mucositis, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, nausea/vomiting, myelosuppression. The lenvatinib+pembrolizumab trial in SGC reported grade 5 events. Cisplatin CRT is well-tolerated in HNSCC but adds substantial morbidity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. High-grade MEC may behave similarly to SCC and respond to platinum-based chemoradiation, but the biology of minor salivary gland tumors differs from mucosal SCC. CRTC1-MAML2 fusion-negative status may indicate more SCC-like behavior.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin acts as a radiosensitizer by forming DNA cross-links that interfere with DNA repair mechanisms, enhancing radiation-induced cell death. Concurrent administration during RT creates synergistic cytotoxicity. Standard dosing: cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 22, 43 of RT course, or weekly 40 mg/m2.

treatments.name: Docetaxel Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Small case series only. In 4 high-grade MEC patients: 2 complete remissions, 2 partial remissions after 6 cycles. Encouraging but extremely limited sample size. No phase II/III data specific to MEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Small series showed 100% response rate in 4 MEC patients, but without survival data or long-term follow-up. Paclitaxel data (related taxane) in MEC showed poor 5-year survival of 0%. Taxane activity in MEC may be transient.

accessibility.rationale: Generic, widely available, inexpensive. Standard oncology infusion. Well-known dosing (75 mg/m2 q3 weeks). No biomarker requirements. Available at any chemotherapy-capable center.

safety_profile.rationale: Standard taxane toxicity: neutropenia (grade 3-4 common), peripheral neuropathy, fluid retention, fatigue, alopecia, nail changes. Requires G-CSF support. Manageable but significant.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. Can be used regardless of molecular profile. Alternative to CAP regimen with potentially less cardiotoxicity (no doxorubicin). Reasonable option if targeted therapies not applicable.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Taxane that binds to beta-tubulin and stabilizes microtubules, preventing depolymerization required for cell division. This blocks mitotic spindle formation, arresting cells in G2/M phase and inducing apoptosis. Active against rapidly dividing tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Docetaxel in High-Grade Mucoepidermoid Carcinoma (Case Series)

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (generic, off-label for salivary gland cancer)

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Vorinostat (PD-1 + HDAC Inhibitor Combination)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial: 25 patients (mixed ACC and non-ACC SGC). ORR 16%, median PFS 6.9 months, median OS 14 months. Epigenetic modulation to enhance immunotherapy response is a novel approach. Limited MEC-specific data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 6.9 months and median OS 14 months represents modest improvement over standard chemotherapy in recurrent SGC. 16% ORR is in line with other ICI combinations. Epigenetic priming may enhance ICI activity.

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab is FDA-approved; vorinostat is FDA-approved for CTCL. Combination is investigational for SGC. May require clinical trial access or off-label justification at academic centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined immune and epigenetic toxicity: immune-related AEs from pembrolizumab plus thrombocytopenia, fatigue, GI toxicity, and dehydration from vorinostat. Manageable but requires close monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not specifically biomarker-selected. Vorinostat may upregulate tumor antigen presentation and PD-L1 expression, enhancing ICI efficacy. MEC immune-inflamed phenotype may benefit from epigenetic priming. Rationale is strongest in tumors with immune infiltrates.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vorinostat is a histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that alters gene expression through epigenetic modification, increasing MHC class I expression and tumor antigen presentation. This epigenetic reprogramming may convert immunologically cold tumors into hot tumors susceptible to PD-1 blockade by pembrolizumab. The combination leverages epigenetic-immune synergy.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Pembrolizumab + Vorinostat in SGC

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing recommended
No specific biomarker requirement for eligibility

treatments.name: Cisplatin + Doxorubicin + Cyclophosphamide (CAP Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Small phase II studies and retrospective series only. No randomized data. Mixed results: ORR 27-60% across studies but short duration of response (3-13 months). High-grade MEC may respond similarly to SCC but 3 MEC patients failed to respond in one series.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS approximately 12 months with CAP regimen. Response duration typically 3-13 months. Short-lived benefit in most patients. Reserved for recurrent/metastatic disease with no targeted therapy options. Not established as adjuvant treatment.

accessibility.rationale: All three drugs are generic, inexpensive, and available worldwide. Standard chemotherapy regimen with well-known dosing and administration protocols. No biomarker requirements.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant toxicity: cardiotoxicity from doxorubicin (cumulative dose-limiting), nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity from cisplatin, hemorrhagic cystitis from cyclophosphamide. Myelosuppression, nausea, alopecia. Grade 3-4 adverse events common.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. May be considered when no targetable alterations are identified. High-grade MEC with fusion-negative status and AR-negative may have limited targeted options, making chemotherapy a fallback. If HER2 FISH is negative, this becomes more relevant.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triple cytotoxic regimen: Cisplatin forms DNA cross-links preventing replication; Doxorubicin intercalates into DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II; Cyclophosphamide is an alkylating agent that cross-links DNA strands. Together they target rapidly dividing cells through complementary mechanisms of DNA damage.

key_evidence.study_name: Cyclophosphamide, Doxorubicin, and Cisplatin in Advanced Salivary Gland Cancer
key_evidence.journal: Archives of Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (individual drugs) — Off-label combination for salivary gland cancer

treatments.name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (VEGFR + PD-1 Combination)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial (NCT04209660) presented at ASCO 2025 with 26 evaluable patients across SGC subtypes. Met primary BOR endpoint. However, significant safety concerns with grade 5 events. Trial included only 2 MEC patients in mixed cohort.

survival_benefit.rationale: Combination showed activity in AcCC cohort. Non-AcCC subtypes showed lower response rates with serious toxicity. Lenvatinib alone achieved 16% ORR and 17.5-month PFS in AcCC. Combination benefit over single agent not clearly established for MEC.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs individually FDA-approved for other indications. Combination used off-label for salivary gland cancers. Available at academic oncology centers. Clinical trial access may be an option.

safety_profile.rationale: Serious safety concerns: 4 possibly treatment-related deaths (3 in SDC cohort, 1 in myoepithelial carcinoma) — respiratory failure from pneumonitis, cardiac arrest, stroke. Grade 3+ AEs common with the combination. Requires very careful monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: MEC has immune-inflamed phenotype and VEGF expression. Combination rationale of anti-angiogenic immunomodulation is sound. However, most activity seen in AcCC, not MEC specifically. No specific biomarker selection available.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFR-alpha, RET, and KIT. It inhibits tumor angiogenesis and modulates the tumor immune microenvironment by reducing immunosuppressive regulatory T cells and tumor-associated macrophages. Combined with pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1), the strategy enhances immune checkpoint blockade in an immunologically primed microenvironment.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab in Recurrent/Metastatic SGC (NCT04209660)

treatments.name: Nintedanib (Multi-Kinase VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial: 20 patients with mixed SGC histologies (2 MEC patients included). Disease control rate 75%, 6-month PFS rate 60%. Small sample size, mixed histologies. No MEC-specific subgroup analysis.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median stable disease duration 8.2 months. Disease stabilization rather than tumor regression. 6-month PFS rate of 60% suggests moderate disease control. No OS data reported.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for IPF and NSCLC (combination). Off-label for SGC. Oral administration. Available at most oncology centers but requires off-label justification.

safety_profile.rationale: Moderate toxicity: diarrhea, nausea, hepatotoxicity (LFT elevation), bleeding risk. Manageable with dose adjustments. Lower toxicity than cytotoxic chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets VEGF pathway which is expressed in MEC. Not biomarker-dependent for eligibility. Broad multi-kinase activity may benefit tumors without specific targetable drivers.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triple angiokinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR 1-3, FGFR 1-3, and PDGFR alpha/beta. Blocks tumor angiogenesis through inhibition of multiple pro-angiogenic receptor tyrosine kinases. Additionally inhibits Src and FLT3 kinases. VEGF and ANG2 are elevated in MEC, providing rationale for anti-angiogenic approach.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Nintedanib in Recurrent/Metastatic SGC

treatments.name: Tipifarnib (Farnesyltransferase Inhibitor — If HRAS Mutant)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II data in HRAS-mutant SGC: 12 evaluable patients, 1 PR (8.3%), 7 SD (58.3%). FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for HRAS-mutant HNSCC. HRAS mutations occur in up to 20% of high-grade SGC subtypes including MEC. Modest single-agent activity.

survival_benefit.rationale: Limited efficacy as monotherapy: 8.3% ORR with disease control rate of 66.7%. Better activity in HRAS-mutant HNSCC (55% ORR). Combination with PI3K inhibitors (alpelisib) shows preclinical synergy via convergent mTOR inhibition. Primarily disease stabilization rather than regression.

accessibility.rationale: Not FDA-approved. Available through clinical trials or expanded access. Requires NGS to confirm HRAS mutation. Limited to specialized academic oncology centers conducting relevant trials.

safety_profile.rationale: Moderate toxicity profile. Main side effects: myelosuppression, GI toxicity, fatigue. Generally manageable. Lower toxicity than cytotoxic chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires HRAS mutation (VAF >20% for optimal response). HRAS mutations found in up to 20% of high-grade MEC. Patient needs NGS testing to determine eligibility. If HRAS wild-type, this option is not applicable.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tipifarnib inhibits farnesyltransferase, an enzyme required for HRAS protein prenylation and membrane localization. By blocking HRAS plasma membrane anchoring, tipifarnib abrogates downstream oncogenic MAPK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling. Uniquely targets HRAS (not KRAS or NRAS) because HRAS solely depends on farnesylation for membrane association.

key_evidence.study_name: Tipifarnib in Recurrent, Metastatic HRAS-Mutant Salivary Gland Cancer

biomarker_requirements: HRAS mutation (preferably VAF >20%)
NGS testing required
HRAS mutations in up to 20% of high-grade MEC

clinical_trials.title: RTOG 1008: Adjuvant Concurrent Radiation and Chemotherapy versus Radiation Alone in Resected High-Risk Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors
sites: NRG Oncology / RTOG network — multi-site US
biomarker_requirements: None — eligibility by stage/grade: T3-4, N1-3, or T1-2 with close/positive margins
clinical_trials.early_results: No published results yet. Randomizes to adjuvant RT (60 Gy) +/- concurrent cisplatin (100 mg/m2 q3 weeks). This is the first randomized trial addressing the role of concurrent chemoRT in salivary gland malignancies. Results will define standard of care for adjuvant treatment.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib Plus Pembrolizumab in Recurrent/Metastatic Salivary Gland Cancers
biomarker_requirements: None — open to all SGC histologies
clinical_trials.early_results: 26 evaluable patients. Met primary BOR endpoint. Active in AcCC cohort. Significant safety concerns with 4 possibly treatment-related deaths (pneumonitis, cardiac arrest, stroke). Less promising for non-AcCC subtypes given toxicity.

clinical_trials.title: DESTINY-PanTumor02: Phase II Study of Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in HER2-Expressing Solid Tumors
biomarker_requirements: HER2 IHC 2+ or 3+ by local or central testing
clinical_trials.early_results: 267 patients total. Salivary gland/HN subset (n=24): ORR 41.7%, with responses lasting >20 months. HER2 IHC 3+ subset: ORR 61.3%, median PFS 11.9 months, median OS 21.1 months. ILD in 10.5% including 3 fatal cases. FDA accelerated approval August 2024 for HER2 IHC 3+ solid tumors.

clinical_trials.title: MYTHOS: Phase II Study of Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in HER2-Positive Recurrent/Metastatic Salivary Gland Cancer
biomarker_requirements: HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+) or HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-)
clinical_trials.early_results: First dedicated phase II of T-DXd in HER2+ SGC. Demonstrated encouraging efficacy and manageable toxicities, supporting T-DXd as effective treatment option in HER2-positive recurrent/metastatic SGC. ILD observed. Full data presented at ESMO 2024.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Nivolumab and Ipilimumab for Metastatic/Recurrent Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma and Other Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors
sites: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, MD Anderson, multi-site US
biomarker_requirements: None — stratified by histology (ACC vs non-ACC)
clinical_trials.early_results: 64 patients (32 ACC, 32 non-ACC). Non-ACC cohort met primary endpoint: ORR 16% (5/32). ACC cohort: ORR 6%. Median PFS: 4.4 months (ACC), 2.2 months (non-ACC). Grade 3+ irAEs in 38%. MEC patients included in non-ACC cohort.

clinical_trials.title: Tipifarnib in Recurrent, Metastatic HRAS-Mutant Salivary Gland Cancer
biomarker_requirements: HRAS mutation (confirmed by NGS)
clinical_trials.early_results: 12 evaluable HRAS-mutant SGC patients: 1 PR (8.3%), 7 SD (58.3%). Disease control rate 66.7%. Modest single-agent activity but promising disease stabilization. HRAS found in up to 20% of high-grade MEC.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Adjuvant Radiation Therapy (60-66 Gy IMRT or Proton)
combination_strategies.evidence_level: Level 2B — NCCN/ASCO Guidelines, Multiple Retrospective Series
combination_strategies.rationale: NCCN guidelines recommend adjuvant RT for all high-grade salivary gland tumors regardless of margin status. Reduces locoregional recurrence from 40-50% to 5-15%. High-grade histology, T2 size, and CRTC1-MAML2 fusion-negative status all independently support adjuvant RT. This is the standard combination for T2N0 high-grade MEC.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Concurrent Cisplatin (100 mg/m2 q3 weeks or 40 mg/m2 weekly)
combination_strategies.rationale: Radiosensitization by cisplatin may improve locoregional control for high-grade SGC, extrapolating from HNSCC data. Taiwan Cancer Registry showed no OS benefit, but some series show improved locoregional control. RTOG 1008 will definitively answer this question. Consider for high-grade fusion-negative MEC given aggressive biology.

T-DXd may induce immunogenic cell death through its cytotoxic payload, potentially synergizing with PD-1 blockade. MEC immune-inflamed microenvironment may enhance this combination. No specific trial data in SGC for this combination, but HER2-directed therapy + ICI combinations are being explored across tumor types.

Tipifarnib potentiates PI3K-alpha inhibition by blocking feedback reactivation of mTOR via convergent HRAS-RHEB-mTOR axis inhibition. Preclinical data in PIK3CA- and HRAS-dysregulated HNSCC shows durable pathway suppression and tumor regression. Particularly relevant if tumors harbor co-occurring PIK3CA and HRAS alterations.

VEGFR inhibition remodels tumor immune microenvironment by reducing suppressive Tregs and promoting dendritic cell maturation, potentially enhancing PD-1 blockade efficacy. Phase II data shows activity in SGC but significant safety concerns. MEC immune-inflamed phenotype may favor this approach. Requires careful patient selection given grade 5 events.

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple institutional series and systematic reviews demonstrate that patients performing range-of-motion (ROM) exercises for jaw, tongue, lips, and larynx in the first 3 months post-surgery show significantly greater improvement in speech and swallowing vs non-exercisers. SLP intervention is standard of care at head and neck cancer centers.
supportive_care.benefit: Preservation and restoration of intelligible speech and safe swallowing function after partial glossectomy. Reduces aspiration risk, improves oral intake, and maintains quality of life. Critical for oral tongue tumors where surgical resection directly impacts these functions.

supportive_care.evidence: ESPEN guidelines for head and neck cancer recommend nutritional assessment at diagnosis, during treatment, and in survivorship. Tongue surgery patients require texture-modified diets. Dietitian-guided intervention reduces weight loss and treatment interruptions.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains adequate caloric intake during treatment and recovery. Prevents malnutrition-related treatment delays. Specialized dietary guidance for post-glossectomy anatomy. May require temporary PEG/NG tube if swallowing severely impaired during RT.

supportive_care.approach: Dental Evaluation and Oral Care (Pre-RT and Ongoing)
supportive_care.evidence: NCCN and ADA guidelines recommend comprehensive dental evaluation before head and neck radiation. Pre-RT dental extractions of compromised teeth reduce osteoradionecrosis risk. Fluoride trays, salivary substitutes, and regular dental follow-up are evidence-based interventions.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents osteoradionecrosis (3-10% risk with IMRT to oral cavity), manages radiation-related dental caries, treats xerostomia symptoms. Custom fluoride carriers reduce caries rate by 50-70%. Critical for oral tongue tumors receiving RT to the oral cavity.

supportive_care.evidence: ACSM Exercise Guidelines for Cancer Survivors (2019) and meta-analyses demonstrate that supervised exercise during and after cancer treatment improves fatigue, physical function, and quality of life. Head and neck cancer-specific exercise programs show reduced treatment-related deconditioning.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces cancer-related fatigue by 25-30%. Improves cardiorespiratory fitness. Maintains lean body mass during treatment. Meta-analyses suggest possible survival benefit with regular physical activity in head and neck cancer survivors. Minimum 150 min/week moderate-intensity activity recommended.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Distress Management Guidelines recommend routine distress screening for all cancer patients. Head and neck cancer patients have among the highest rates of depression (20-40%) and anxiety. Cognitive behavioral therapy and supportive counseling show efficacy in reducing psychological distress.
supportive_care.benefit: Early identification and treatment of depression and anxiety. Addresses body image concerns specific to oral cancer. Improves treatment adherence and quality of life. Young patient age (45) and visible tumor site increase psychosocial impact.

supportive_care.evidence: Amifostine (radioprotectant) reduces RT-induced xerostomia per ASCO guidelines. Pilocarpine and cevimeline are FDA-approved sialagogues. Proton beam therapy significantly reduces contralateral salivary gland dose vs IMRT. Salivary substitutes and humidifiers provide symptom relief.
supportive_care.benefit: Preserves remaining salivary function during RT. Reduces chronic xerostomia (major quality of life impact). Proton therapy reduces xerostomia incidence vs IMRT. Sialagogues improve saliva production in 40-50% of patients with residual gland function.
